summary: German philosopher and Young Hegelian who wrote The Essence of Christianity, arguing that 'theology is anthropology' - the concept of God is an anthropomorphic projection of the mind. Known as the bridge between Hegel and Marx, and as the subject of Marx's 11 Theses on Feuerbach.

works.name: The Essence of Christianity
works.description: Feuerbach's most famous work argues that religious ideals are externalizations of features inherent in humans, and that the reverence we have for God is really reverence for Man the species. It draws on D.F. Strauss's The Life of Jesus Critically Examined and proclaims 'theology is anthropology.' The book identifies humanity itself as the 'essence of Christianity' and claims belief in revelation 'poisons' the innate moral sense. Max Stirner responded to it in The Ego and Its Own, and Bruno Bauer attacked it in an essay on Feuerbach's 'Characteristics.' It strongly influenced Marx's theory of alienation.

clues.clue: 'Theology is anthropology' - God is an anthropomorphic projection of the mind; religious ideals are externalizations of human features
examples: view that humans created the concept of God by elevating abstract human characteristics led to his proclamation that 'theology is anthropology'
religious ideals are simply externalizations of features already inherent in humans

examples: draws heavily on D.F. Strauss' The Life of Jesus Critically Examined

examples: claims belief in revelation 'poisons' the innate moral sense of people and identifies humanity itself as the title concept

clues.clue: Strongly influenced Marx's theory of alienation
examples: The Essence of Christianity strongly influenced this man, who used Feuerbach's ideas in his Theory of Alienation

works.name: Marx's Theses on Feuerbach
works.description: Marx wrote 11 short Theses on Feuerbach, the most famous being the 11th: 'Philosophers have only interpreted the world; the point is to change it.' The Theses criticize Feuerbach for restricting human essence to the individual rather than seeing it as a product of social relations, and for privileging theoretical activity over practice. They served as an outline for The German Ideology. Althusser considered the Theses as the marker of Marx's break from the 'Young' to the 'Mature' period.

clues.clue: Subject of Marx's 11 Theses: 'Philosophers have only interpreted the world; the point is to change it'
examples: The subject of eleven short aphorisms, the last of which states, 'Philosophers have only interpreted the world'
Marx wrote a set of 11 short observations titled for this man

clues.clue: Marx criticized Feuerbach for restricting human essence to the individual; conceiving practice 'only in its dirty Jewish manifestation'
examples: disagrees with the other's contention that human nature is an 'abstraction inherent in each single individual'

clues.clue: Theses served as outline for The German Ideology's first chapter
examples: The 'Theses on Feuerbach' served as an outline for the first chapter of this set of manuscripts

clues.clue: Althusser: Marx's break from Young to Mature coincided with split from Feuerbach
examples: Althusser considered Marx's break from Young to Mature to coincide with his split with this other thinker

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Feuerbach was a Young Hegelian who bridges Hegel and Marx. He argued for a new philosophy grounded in sensation in Principles for the Philosophy of the Future. His late work (On Spiritualism and Materialism; On Moral Philosophy) focused on the drive toward happiness. His review of Moleschott's book The Theory of Food yielded the punning dictum 'Man is what he eats' (a pun only in German: 'ist was er isst'). He also wrote that self-consciousness dependent on nature is the defining characteristic of mankind, declaring the impossibility of a 'religion of the elephants.' Marx and Feuerbach appear together as a tossup answer about the cherry tree argument. Althusser also studied Feuerbach in the late 1950s.

clues.clue: Young Hegelian who bridges Hegel and Marx
examples: this philosopher's thought bridges that of his teacher Hegel and a younger thinker

examples: review of his student Jacob Moleschotts's book The Theory of Food is the origin of the punning dictum 'Man is what he eats'

clues.clue: Principles for the Philosophy of the Future: new philosophy grounded in sensation vs. Cartesian rationalism
examples: argued for a new philosophy grounded in sensation in opposition to Cartesian rationalism in his Principles for the Philosophy of the Future

examples: touts the impossibility of a 'religion of the elephants' in stating that self-consciousness dependent on nature is the defining characteristic

examples: his late work, including the essay 'On Spiritualism and Materialism,' and the book On Moral Philosophy, was chiefly concerned with the drive towards happiness

examples: Althusser's thought in the late 1950s, which culminated in Althusser's publication in 1960 of an essay on this thinker's 'Philosophical Manifestoes'

comprehensive_summary:
Ludwig Feuerbach appears in quizbowl as a German philosopher and Young Hegelian who bridges the thought of Hegel and Marx. His most famous work, The Essence of Christianity, argues that religious ideals are externalizations of features inherent in humans, proclaiming 'theology is anthropology.' The concept of God is an anthropomorphic projection of the mind, and reverence for God is really reverence for Man the species. The work draws on D.F. Strauss's The Life of Jesus Critically Examined and claims belief in revelation 'poisons' the innate moral sense. It was attacked by Max Stirner in The Ego and Its Own and by Bruno Bauer.

Feuerbach is perhaps best known through Marx's 11 Theses on Feuerbach, the last of which declares 'Philosophers have only interpreted the world; the point is to change it.' Marx criticized Feuerbach for restricting human essence to the individual and for privileging theoretical activity. The Theses served as an outline for The German Ideology. Althusser considered the Theses as marking Marx's transition from 'Young' to 'Mature' philosophy.

Biographically, Feuerbach coined the punning dictum 'Man is what he eats' (a pun only in German: 'ist was er isst') from a review of Moleschott's book. He argued for a philosophy grounded in sensation in Principles for the Philosophy of the Future, and his late works focused on the drive toward happiness. He declared the impossibility of a 'religion of the elephants,' stating that self-consciousness dependent on nature defines mankind. The Essence of Christianity strongly influenced Marx's theory of alienation.
